title: Project description
description: Providing a project abstract, project proposal or other project description puts the DMP in context and is thus useful information for anyone reading or evaluating the DMP.

title: Who can access the archived data
Are the criteria for application to access the data openly available?
Is access to the data/record/source restricted or needs to be applied for?
How will re-users be able to get access?
description: Consider that users of a data set will need to be able to get access several years after the project is finished. The best way to arrange this is to put the responsibility for checking the rights to access the data with a fixed body in an organisation (a data access committee), rather than a single contact person (who might be unavailable in the future).

title: Will you need to add fields in your data format to a data type registry?
description: Even if the data format you are using is unique to your project, the discrete data items should be reused or reusable as much as possible. Data type registries can help with that.

title: Could you use data aggregation to make the data openly available?
description: Aggregated data, where typically at least 15 individuals are in any data point, are considered sufficiently anonymous. This is an alternative way of making data openly available for future research.

title: Could anonymization be used to make the data more openly available?
description: Different anonymization techniques exist. Disadvantage of anonymization is that data integration becomes virtually impossible, but it may be the only way to open up your data for other research.

Will a license be assigned to the data as early as possible?
description: It is not always clear to everyone in the project (or outside it) what can and can not be done with a dataset. It is therefore helpful to associate each dataset with a license as early as possible in the project. The license should be machine-readable wherever possible. A data license should ideally be as little restrictive as possible.

title: Will you be keeping a master list with checksums of data?
description: Data corruption or mistakes can happen with large amounts of files or large files. Keeping a master list with data checksums can be helpful to prevent expensive mistakes, because it will help detect early when data files are damaged or mixed up. It can also be helpful to keep the list under version control so that all changes are well documented.

The same strategies for linking metadata should be communicated to all project members who manages data in the project. Multiple selections will be relevant in most projects.

title: Does this dataset contain personal data?
description: Is there anything in this dataset that could be tied to a person? This could be a physical characteristic, but also behavior of a person, movements, communications. Note that e.g. readouts about the performance of an airplane are considered to contain personal data of the pilot! The GDPR states that the processing (including storing) of personal data should stop when the intended purpose of the processing is done. There are, however, exemptions to this e.g. when the processing is done for research purposes. Also, from a research ethics point of view, research data should be kept to make it possible for others to validate published research findings and reuse data for new discoveries. This is also governed by what the data subjects have been informed about regarding how you will treat the data after project completion. The recommendation is to deposit the sensitive data in the appropriate controlled access repositories if such are available, but this requires that the data subjects are informed and have agreed to this.

title: Does this dataset contain sensitive information?
description: Personal information can be sensitive if it is for instance about the health, sexual orientation, religion of a person. But there are also other classes of sensitive information: e.g. locations of rare species in biodiversity could be sensitive and should not leak to poachers.

All projects should at least have a Contact Person and a Project Leader. A person can have several roles. In educational settings, the supervisor(s) should be specified as they have formal responsibility for data management.

description: For good interoperability the use of controlled vocabularies for each discrete data item is advisable. If such vocabularies exist, it is best to reuse those.

title: Data documentation during the project
description: This chapter collects information about how metadata and other accompanying information will be handled in the active phase of the project. The planned use of metadata will benefit the research project both internally, among project members, as well as help make the data understandable and FAIR for external users and future reuse. Supporting information for this chapter

title: Will you use a database system to store project data?
Who are the data controller(s) of the personal data processed in the project?
description: Article 4 (7): “ ‘controller’ means the natural or legal person, public authority, agency or other body which, alone or jointly with others, determines the purposes and means of the processing of personal data; […].” The Controller is typically the university employer of the PI, and the PI should act as a representative of her university employer and is responsible for ensuring that personal data is handled correctly in her projects. For cross-border collaborative projects the controllers of different datasets should be identified. Also, if joint controllership is considered, make sure that all parties understand their obligations, and it is probably good to define the terms for this in an agreement between the parties.

title: Description of the data to be worked on collaboratively
description: Briefly describe the data to be worked on directly in a shared working space. If applicable, you can refer to information provided in the previous chapters. When making the work space, it helps to know whether you expect to work with very many small files, a few very large files, whether you will use a (SQL) database to store most of the data. Maybe in order to carry out your work you have to consider specialised storage systems for parallelised access? Such information can be collected here.

description: There are many factors that can contribute to the risk of information loss or information leaks. They are often part of the behavior of the people that are involved in the project, but can also be steered by properly planned infrastructure.

title: How do you prevent loss of data in the work space?

description: What is considered a set/collection of data may be structured according to the needs of the project. A logical unit can for example be all data recorded by a given instrument, or all measurements undertaken in a given setting or on a given set of samples.

title: Have data processing agreements been (or will they be) established between the data controller(s) and any data processors?
description: Note: A data processing agreement has to contain the obligatory clauses specified in Art 28.3 of the GDPR. The agreement should also regulate the use of any sub-processors. Article 4 (8): “ ‘processor’ means a natural or legal person, public authority, agency or other body which processes personal data on behalf of the controller.” Examples of this is if you use a secure computing environment provided by another organisation to do your analysis or to store the data, along with several other scenarios. In the case that you do, there needs to be a legal agreement established between the controller(s) and processor(s) as defined in Article 28 (3): “Processing by a processor shall be governed by a contract or other legal act under Union or Member State law, that is binding on the processor with regard to the controller and that sets out the subject-matter and duration of the processing, the nature and purpose of the processing, the type of personal data and categories of data subjects and the obligations and rights of the controller. […]” Article 28 also lists the required contents of such an agreement. Your organisation and/or the processor organisation will probably have agreement templates that you can use.

What are the exemptions to the prohibition for processing of special categories of data (such as health and genetic data) under Art. 9 GDPR used? State cohort/dataset for each type of exemption.
description: Processing of certain categories of personal data is not allowed unless there are exemptions in law to allow this. Among these categories (“sensitive data”) are “‘[...] data revealing racial or ethnic origin, [...] genetic data, [...] data concerning health’” . Most types of personal data collected in biomedical research will fall under these categories. Article 9 (2) lists a number of exemptions that apply, of which consent and scientific research are most likely to be relevant for research. Please consult with your Data Protection Officer of your organisation.

title: Under what GDPR category does the project data fall?
description: The GDPR defines (sub)categories for personal data and expects data processors to be aware of and to document the categories of data that they process. Standard personal data includes e.g. name, date of birth, address, (online) identifiers, contact info, account/personnel/customer or other ID numbers, appearance. Special category data includes: data concerning health, Genetic data, racial or ethnic origin, sexual orientation, political opinions, religious or ideological convictions or trade union membership, criminal convictions and offences

description: Anonymisation refers to the processing of personal data in a manner that makes it impossible to identify individuals from them. Pseudonymisation refers to the processing of personal data in such a manner that the personal data can no longer be attributed to a specific person without the use of additional information, i.e. any identifying characteristics are replaced with a pseudonym.

What legal basis are you using to collect and process the personal data?
description: Article 6 (1) lists under what conditions the processing is considered lawful. Of these, Consent or Public interest are relevant when it comes to research. You should determine what legal basis (or bases) you have for processing the personal data in your project. Traditionally, consent has been the basis for processing personal data for research, but under the GDPR there cannot be an imbalance between the processor and the data subject for it to be considered to be freely given. In some countries the use of consent as the legal basis for processing by universities for research purposes is therefore not recommended. In those cases, public interest should probably be your legal basis. Note that if your legal basis for processing is consent, a number of requirements exists for the consent to be considered valid under the GDPR. Consents given before the GDPR might not live up to this. Also note that even if public interest is the legal basis, other laws and research ethics standards might still require you to have consent from the subjects for performing the research. Please consult with the Data Protection Officer of your organisation on which legal basis to apply to your data.

title: Do suitable metadata standards/schemas exist for the data?
description: A metadata standard/schema is a predefined set of attributes to describe data in a clear and consistent way. Metadata standards/schemas can be generic or discipline-specific, and research communities have worked together to define what type of metadata are needed when research of a certain kind is performed and described. Complete metadata helps to organise data during the project, and is necessary for data archiving.

Which other?
description: The following legal bases are unlikely for processing personal data when doing research. The meaning of the different options are as follows: Fulfil contract: we require the processing in order to fulfil our contract with the data subjects. Legitimate interest: data subjects all expect us to do this data processing because of who we are. Vital interest: we need to do this to protect the vital interests of the data subject. Legal requirement: we are legally obliged to do this data processing.

title: Will any personal data be collected/processed?
description: Personal data is any information that can be linked to a person. If there is a way for someone, including yourself, to find out who that person is, that is considered personal data. This includes both data identifying a person directly, and data that allows indirect identification by combining different pieces of information. Examples of personal data are: name; birth day; address; a voice recording; a combination of a location and a time (traffic flow); genetic information; an X-ray of the skull.

Privacy considerations
description: All processing of personal data must be in accordance with The Personal Data Act (Norwegian: Personopplysningsloven), which incorporates the EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) in Norway. If you work with health data, compliance with the Health research Act also affect how the personal data can be processed. If you already described the processing of personal data in detail in other systems including local registration of personal data processing (e.g. RETTE at UiB), the Sikt notification form for personal data ('Sikt Meldeskjema') or executing and registering a Data Protection Impact assessment (DPIA), you can refer to these registration(s). The step-by-step dialogue guides you through relevant considerations on legal processing of personal data. Be aware that you might have to register processing of personal data in additional systems as required by your institution. There is no information exchange between the DMP and these systems.

Many projects make use of datasets published in discipline-specific, institutional and generic research-data archives, registry data, or so-called "reference data" which is used by many projects. As many of the same considerations apply, also access to materials that are commonly referred to as 'records' or 'sources' rather than 'data' should be included here, such as material in public archives, media archives, legal resources, or large amounts of digital literature. description: There are surprisingly many complications that can cause (slight) inconsistencies between results when workflows are run on different compute infrastructures. This question addresses common approaches to mitigate this.

title: How long will this data set be kept?
description: For optimum reusability data needs to be available for as long as possible. Yet, there may be financial or other reasons to delete the data at a given point.

title: Will the consent form be available for re-users?
description: It is important for re-users to know what consent items you asked exactly from participants. Note that the answers from participants is personal data that requires protection!

title: Reusing and collecting data
description: This chapter collects information about the type and origin of the data that will be included in the project. This could be both datasets and records originating outside the project, as well as novel data collected, captured, generated or created by you or other project members. Different disciplines may use different terms to describe the data or records underlying a research project. In this chapter, an inclusive approach is used with 'data' functioning as a broad and overarching term. This includes observational and experimental data, surveys, registry data, simulations, as well as data in digital museum archives and other data records. Supporting information for this chapter

title: What technical and procedural safeguards have been established for processing the data?
description: To ensure that the personal data that you process in the project is protected at an appropriate level, you should apply technical and procedural safeguards to ensure that the rights of the data subjects are not violated. Examples of such measures include, but are not limited to, pseudonymisation and encryption of data, the use of computing and storage environments with heightened security, and clear and documented procedures for project members to follow.

chapter: Storing and protecting data during the project
description: List all storage and computing solutions that you are planning to use in the course of the project. Consider all digital data that you are expecting to handle in the course of the project, including files only kept temporary (e.g. intermediate computational/analysis results) and working documents (e.g. manuscripts, electronic lab notebooks). Examples: Local hardware Institutionally managed cloud storage (e.g. OneDrive, Educloud operated by UiO) Institutionally managed data servers or network drives Institutionally managed cold storage (tape storage) Trusted research environments (TSD provided by UiO, HUNT cloud provided by NTNU, SAFE provided by UiB) National storage infrastructure (e.g. NIRD provided by Sigma2) HPC infrastructure (e.g. NREC operated by UiB and UiO) Other cloud storage (e.g. Google Drive, Dropbox) Online version control services (e.g. GitHub, GitLab) Online document services (e.g. Overleaf, Googledocs) Online platforms offering software-as-a-service

title: What kind of training?
Do you plan to transfer data outside the EU/EEA?
description: Where personal data are transferred to a third country or to an international organization, appropriate measures (technical and organizational) must be put in place to protect the data. If sufficient protection levels are not offered by the countries, the addition of specific data protection clauses must be added to contracts. Keep in mind that a processor shall not transfer data outside the EU/EEA without the data controller’s approval and instructions, and that it should be subject to contractual agreements as imposed by the data controller.

title: Can your work flow and work flow system make use of "trivial" parallelism in the computations?
description: If you need to perform many similar calculations of different input data (e.g. samples or simulations), these can often be executed at the same time on different nodes in a compute system. This is sometimes called "trivial parallelism". Even though it is named "trivial", this still requires that the work flow and workflow system support this (e.g. by making sure all file names are unique, and that not many threads are trying to read or write the same file at the same time).

title: Can you make use of an existing 'trusted third party' for pseudonymization?
description: Making use of the same pseudonymization for different studies makes it possible to integrate information later. Obviously it also raises the risk of re-identification.
